title: Bronchiectasis
oneLiner: Chronic dilation of bronchi from recurrent infection/inflammation, causing chronic cough with sputum and recurrent exacerbations.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old woman with a 20-year history of recurrent “chest infections” presents with daily cough producing copious purulent sputum (up to 200 mL/day) and progressive dyspnoea. She has had three hospital admissions for pneumonia in the last 2 years. On auscultation, you hear coarse inspiratory crackles in both bases. Chest X-ray shows “tram-track” opacities and ring shadows.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Daily cough with copious purulent sputum** → excessive mucus production from damaged airways; hallmark of bronchiectasis.
- **Recurrent “chest infections”** → impaired mucociliary clearance leads to repeated infection, often with same organism.
- **Multiple admissions for pneumonia** → structural lung damage predisposes to severe infections; key clue for chronicity.
- **Coarse inspiratory crackles** → excess secretions in dilated bronchi; crackles are coarse and clear with cough (unlike fibrosis).
- **Tram-track and ring shadows on CXR** → parallel thickened bronchial walls (tram-tracks) and dilated airways seen en face (rings).
- **Progressive dyspnoea** → lung parenchymal destruction, airflow obstruction, and exacerbation-driven decline.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **“Dry” bronchiectasis** – scanty sputum, but recurrent haemoptysis (from bronchial artery hypertrophy). Often mistaken for bronchial adenoma or TB.
- **Early, asymptomatic** – found incidentally on CT for another reason; patient denies symptoms but may have subtle cough.
- **Post-tuberculosis** – patient with old TB scar on CXR, now with increasing cough and weight loss; consider non-cavitary bronchiectasis behind fibrotic apex.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Bronchiectasis is a permanent, abnormal widening of the bronchi due to damage to the airway walls from recurrent or severe infections. The widened airways lose their ability to clear mucus, leading to a vicious cycle of mucus stasis, bacterial colonisation, and further inflammation.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Increasingly recognised (UK prevalence ~500/100,000), especially in elderly, COPD, and post-TB populations.
- **Stakes:** Patients have poor quality of life, frequent hospitalisations, and a 5-year mortality of 20–30% (worse than many cancers).
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing bronchiectasis with COPD or asthma – treatment differs (e.g., avoid inhaled corticosteroids in most exacerbations). Missed diagnosis delays airway clearance and increases antibiotic resistance.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of disease:**  
It starts with a **“hit”** that damages the bronchial wall – often a severe infection (e.g., pertussis, measles, adenovirus) or a predisposing condition (cystic fibrosis, immunodeficiency). The inflammation recruits neutrophils, which release elastase and other proteases. These enzymes degrade the **elastic fibres** and **smooth muscle** of the bronchial walls. Over time, the airway loses its recoil and dilates permanently.

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- **Dilated bronchi** → **Tram-tracks on CXR** (thickened, parallel bronchial walls) and **air–fluid levels** on CT (dilated sacs).
- **Mucus stasis** → **Chronic cough with sputum** – the body tries to clear the accumulated secretions.
- **Neutrophillic inflammation** → **Coarse crackles** – secretions and airway wall thickening cause repetitive popping sounds during inspiration.
- **Bronchial artery hypertrophy** → **Haemoptysis** (especially in “dry” form) – fragile new vessels bleed easily.
- **Airflow obstruction** → **Dyspnoea** – from mucus plugging and airway collapse.
- **Repeated infections** → **Exacerbations** – bacterial overgrowth (often *Haemophilus influenzae*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*).
- **Progressive lung destruction** → **Chest deformity** (Harrison’s sulci in children) and **digital clubbing** (chronic infection).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | ~50% of cases | No clear cause after full workup; often bilateral lower lobe |
| **Post-infectious** | Severe pneumonia (TB, pertussis, measles, adenovirus) | History of pneumonia, TB contact, or childhood infection |
| **Immunodeficiency** | Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), IgG subclass deficiency | Recurrent sinopulmonary infections, low immunoglobulins |
| **Autoimmune / connective tissue disease** | Rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren’s, IB* | Arthritis, dry eyes/mouth, IBD |
| **Genetic** | *Cystic fibrosis (CF)* – most common genetic cause | Pancreatic insufficiency, male infertility, sweat test >60 mmol/L |
| **Ciliary dyskinesia** | Primary ciliary dyskinesia (e.g., Kartagener’s) | Situs inversus, infertility, chronic sinusitis |
| **Aspiration** | Recurrent aspiration (GERD, dysphagia) | History of stroke, dementia, hiatus hernia |
| **Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)** | Asthma with ABPA | High IgE, Aspergillus skin test positive, fleeting shadows on CXR |
| **Obstruction** | Lung mass, foreign body | Unilateral, focal bronchiectasis on CT |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (50%). In children and young adults, consider CF.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Diagnosis is **radiologic** – high-resolution CT (HRCT) showing bronchiectasis (bronchial diameter > adjacent artery, lack of tapering, or visualisation of airways within 1 cm of pleural surface). There is no single scoring system; severity classified by number of lobes affected and presence of *Pseudomonas*.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **HRCT chest** | Bronchial dilation, bronchial wall thickening, mucus plugging | Gold standard to confirm diagnosis | Positive if bronchoarterial ratio >1 | 
| **Sputum culture** | Pathogens (e.g., *H. influenzae*, *P. aeruginosa*, *S. aureus*, NTM) | Guide antibiotic choice; identify colonisation | If growth of *P. aeruginosa* → worse prognosis, needs anti-pseudomonal regimen |
| **Chest X-ray** | Tram-tracks, ring shadows, volume loss | Initial screening (low sensitivity ~40%) | Normal CXR does not rule out bronchiectasis |
| **Full blood count** | Neutrophilia (infection), anaemia (chronic disease) | Exclude underlying anaemia; acute exacerbation | Raised WCC with neutrophilia suggests infection |
| **Immunoglobulins / B-cell subsets** | Low IgG (CVID), low subclasses | Detect immunodeficiency | IgG <4 g/L suggests CVID; IgG subclass deficiency common |
| **Sweat chloride** | Chloride >60 mmol/L | Screen for CF in adults <40 or young-onset | Abnormal → refer for CFTR genetics |
| **AE: CRP, blood cultures** | Raised CRP, bacteraemia | Assess severity of exacerbation | CRP >100 mg/L associated with poor outcome |
| **Lung function** | Obstructive pattern (reduced FEV1, FEV1/FVC <0.7) | Monitor disease progression | FEV1 decline >30 mL/year is rapid; correlates with mortality |
| **Bronchoscopy** (selective) | Visualises endobronchial obstruction, obtains BAL | If foreign body, tumour, or suspected ABPA | Lavage culture may isolate unsuspected NTM |

**Single finding that confirms:** HRCT showing bronchial dilation without tapering. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal HRCT chest (with no bronchiectasis in any lobe).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – acute severe exacerbation:**
- **Oxygen** – target SpO2 88–92% (avoid hyperoxia if at risk of hypercapnia).
- **Antibiotics** – if purulent sputum and fever:  
  - For mild exacerbation: *amoxicillin 500 mg PO TDS 7–10 days*  
  - If penicillin allergic: *doxycycline 200 mg PO stat, then 100 mg OD*  
  - If *Pseudomonas* known or recent (last culture): *ciprofloxacin 750 mg PO BD*  
- **Airway clearance** – start physiotherapy with active cycle of breathing (ACBT) **immediately** – do not wait for stabilisation.
- **Salbutamol nebulised 2.5–5 mg** – if wheeze or FEV1 response known.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – management of exacerbation and recovery:**
- Repeat sputum culture – adjust antibiotics based on sensitivities. If no improvement in 48 h, consider IV therapy: *ceftazidime 2 g IV TDS* or *piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS*.
- **Chest physiotherapy** TID – postural drainage, percussion, positive expiratory pressure (PEP) device.
- **Assess for complications:** persistent fever → infected cyst imaging repeat CXR; haemoptysis → consider bronchial artery embolisation if >300 mL/24h.
- **Treat underlying cause** – start *IVIG* if CVID; *corticosteroids* if ABPA (prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day tapering); *domperidone + PPIs* if aspiration.
- **NOT to do:** Do NOT give high-dose systemic steroids routinely (risk of infection augmentation). Do NOT use inhaled corticosteroids alone (no strong evidence, increased pneumonia risk).

**Long-term (chronic management):**
- **Airway clearance** – daily physiotherapy (PEP device or Autogenic drainage). Consider mucolytics: *carbocisteine 750 mg TDS* or *hypertonic saline 6% nebulised BD* if thick sputum.
- **Prophylactic antibiotics** – if ≥3 exacerbations/year:  
  - Rotating oral antibiotics (e.g., *azithromycin 250 mg PO three times per week*).  
  - Or inhaled *colistin* or *tobramycin* if *Pseudomonas* colonised.
- **Vaccinations** – annual influenza, pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPV23) once, and later PPV23 booster.
- **Surgery** – consider for localised bronchiectasis (e.g., post-obstructive) with recurrent severe infections despite medical therapy. Resection can cure if focal.
- **Monitor** – FEV1 annually, sputum cultures twice yearly, repeat HRCT if new symptoms.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Bronchiectasis | COPD | Asthma | Cystic Fibrosis |
|---------|----------------|------|--------|-----------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none; pleuritic if pneumothorax | Rare; tightness | Rare; chest tightness | None; sinus pain possible |
| **Exam** | Coarse crackles, clubbing (20%), wheeze (if obstruction) | Hyperinflation, wheeze, prolonged expiration | Expiratory wheeze, normal or hyperinflated | Crackles, clubbing, hyperinflation, nasal polyps |
| **CXR/CT** | Tram-tracks, ring shadows, air–fluid levels | Hyperinflation, bullae | Normal or hyperinflation | Bronchiectasis (upper lobe predominant), mucus plugging |
| **Sputum** | Purulent, daily, often positive for *H. influenzae* or *P. aeruginosa* | Mucoid, less volume, often sterile | Clear, scanty | Purulent, thick, often *S. aureus* or *P. aeruginosa* |
| **Allergy** | Not typical; ABPA can coexist | Not typical | Positive skin prick test, IgE | Not typical |
| **Key differentiator** | **HRCT shows bronchial dilation** | No bronchiectasis on HRCT; smoking hx | Variable symptoms, normal CT | **Sweat chloride >60**; CFTR mutation |
| **Response to steroids** | Minimal (unless ABPA) | Good (improves FEV1) | Excellent (reversible obstruction) | Minimal (unless ABPA) |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Exacerbation** | Increased cough, purulence, dyspnoea, fever | Worsening crackles, increased sputum volume, fever | Start antibiotics, airway clearance |
| **Massive haemoptysis** | Sudden coughing up >300 mL bright red blood | Hypoxaemia, hypotension, respiratory distress | Urgent bronchial artery embolisation (call interventional radiology), airway protection (intubation) |
| **Pneumothorax** | Acute pleuritic chest pain, dyspnoea | Unilateral reduced breath sounds, tracheal deviation if tension | Chest drain (small → aspirate; large → drain) |
| **Respiratory failure** | Severe dyspnoea, confusion, mottled skin | Low SpO2, high pCO2, acidosis | **Escalate to ICU** for non-invasive ventilation or mechanical ventilation |
| **Bronchiectasis-related lung abscess** | Persistent fever despite antibiotics, weight loss | Localised crackles, HRCT shows cavity | CT-guided drainage or surgical lobectomy |
| **Cor pulmonale** | Fatigue, peripheral oedema, raised JVP | Right ventricular heave, loud P2, hepatomegaly | Diuretics, oxygen therapy, treat underlying lung disease |
| **Pulmonary hypertension** | Exertional syncope, chest pain, haemoptysis | Loud P2, right axis deviation on ECG | Echocardiogram; refer to specialist |
| **Death** | Progressive decline, recurrent severe exacerbations | Cachexia, low FEV1, frequent hospitalisation | Palliative care; consider lung transplant |

**Late-presentation patterns – “what happens if untreated”:**
- Untreated massive haemoptysis → exsanguination → death.
- Repeated exacerbations without airway clearance → progressive loss of lung function → respiratory failure.
- Missed underlying immunodeficiency → recurrent infections → permanent lung damage.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
1. **“BRONCHIECTASIS”** – for causes:  
   - **B** – Bronchial obstruction (tumour, foreign body)  
   - **R** – Rheumatoid / RA  
   - **O** – Obstruction (post-infectious)  
   - **N** – NTM (non-tuberculous mycobacteria)  
   - **C** – Cystic fibrosis  
   - **H** – Hypogammaglobulinaemia  
   - **I** – Idiopathic (most common)  
   - **E** – Eing (ABPA)  
   - **C** – Ciliary dyskinesia  
   - **T** – TB  
   - **A** – Aspiration  
   - **S** – Swyer-James syndrome  
   - **I** – Irritants (toxic gases)  
   - **S** – Sternomastoid? (just a filler, but remember idiopathic)
2. **“PILLAR”** – for management pillars:  
   - **P** – Physiotherapy (airway clearance)  
   - **I** – Infective exacerbations (treat early)  
   - **L** – Lifestyle (vaccinations, exercise)  
   - **L** – Lung damage monitoring (HRCT, FEV1)  
   - **A** – Antibiotics (prophylactic if needed)  
   - **R** – Referral (surgery, transplant)

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **“Dry” bronchiectasis → think TB** – fibrosis and traction bronchiectasis in upper lobes.
2. **Clubbing + crackles** = think bronchiectasis (clubbing rare in uncomplicated COPD).
3. **Sputum colour: “pea soup” green** suggests *Pseudomonas*.
4. **Recurrent pneumonia same lobe** → think focal bronchiectasis from obstruction.
5. **CT shows “signet rings”** (dilated bronchus next to pulmonary artery) – pathognomonic.
6. **Inhaled corticosteroids increase pneumonia risk** – avoid in bronchiectasis without asthma/ABPA.
7. **Ciprofloxacin** is the only oral anti-pseudomonal – use sparingly; resistance develops fast.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Missing bronchiectasis as a cause of “COPD exacerbation” → fails to initiate airway clearance.
- Treating exacerbations with amoxicillin alone when *Pseudomonas* is known → leads to failure.
- Forgetting to culture sputum before antibiotics → cannot target therapy.
- Neglecting physiotherapy → patient stales and re-admission.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Parameter | When to escalate | To whom | Action |
|-----------|------------------|---------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Any single red (respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92% on room air, or any oxygen) | Senior clinician / med reg | Immediate review, consider escalation |
| **GCS <15** | Drowsiness, confusion – may be hypercapnic (pCO2 >6 kPa) | ICU outreach / respiratory team | Check ABG, start NIV if pCO2 >6 + pH <7.35 |
| **Massive haemoptysis** | >300 mL/24h or any haemoptysis with hypotension | Interventional radiology (embolisation) | Organise CT angiography; call IR; prepare for intubation |
| **Lactate >2 mmol/L** | Sepsis from pneumonia or lung abscess | ICU | Start IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, consider critical care |
| **Respiratory arrest** | Apnoea, gasping, bradycardia | Crash team (2222) | Start resuscitation, airway support |
| **Pneumothorax (tension)** | Unilateral hyper-resonance + tracheal deviation + hypotension | Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL) | Then chest drain |

**General rule:** Any patient with bronchiectasis who has a sudden deterioration in oxygen saturations or new confusion needs **urgent ABG** and **senior review** – hypercapnic respiratory failure can be silent.

rawMarkdown:
## Bronchiectasis
**One-liner:** Chronic dilation of bronchi from recurrent infection/inflammation, causing chronic cough with sputum and recurrent exacerbations.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old woman with a 20-year history of recurrent “chest infections” presents with daily cough producing copious purulent sputum (up to 200 mL/day) and progressive dyspnoea. She has had three hospital admissions for pneumonia in the last 2 years. On auscultation, you hear coarse inspiratory crackles in both bases. Chest X-ray shows “tram-track” opacities and ring shadows.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Daily cough with copious purulent sputum** → excessive mucus production from damaged airways; hallmark of bronchiectasis.
- **Recurrent “chest infections”** → impaired mucociliary clearance leads to repeated infection, often with same organism.
- **Multiple admissions for pneumonia** → structural lung damage predisposes to severe infections; key clue for chronicity.
- **Coarse inspiratory crackles** → excess secretions in dilated bronchi; crackles are coarse and clear with cough (unlike fibrosis).
- **Tram-track and ring shadows on CXR** → parallel thickened bronchial walls (tram-tracks) and dilated airways seen en face (rings).
- **Progressive dyspnoea** → lung parenchymal destruction, airflow obstruction, and exacerbation-driven decline.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **“Dry” bronchiectasis** – scanty sputum, but recurrent haemoptysis (from bronchial artery hypertrophy). Often mistaken for bronchial adenoma or TB.
- **Early, asymptomatic** – found incidentally on CT for another reason; patient denies symptoms but may have subtle cough.
- **Post-tuberculosis** – patient with old TB scar on CXR, now with increasing cough and weight loss; consider non-cavitary bronchiectasis behind fibrotic apex.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:** Bronchiectasis is a permanent, abnormal widening of the bronchi due to damage to the airway walls from recurrent or severe infections. The widened airways lose their ability to clear mucus, leading to a vicious cycle of mucus stasis, bacterial colonisation, and further inflammation.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Increasingly recognised (UK prevalence ~500/100,000), especially in elderly, COPD, and post-TB populations.
- **Stakes:** Patients have poor quality of life, frequent hospitalisations, and a 5-year mortality of 20–30% (worse than many cancers).
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing bronchiectasis with COPD or asthma – treatment differs (e.g., avoid inhaled corticosteroids in most exacerbations). Missed diagnosis delays airway clearance and increases antibiotic resistance.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**Story of disease:**  
It starts with a **“hit”** that damages the bronchial wall – often a severe infection (e.g., pertussis, measles, adenovirus) or a predisposing condition (cystic fibrosis, immunodeficiency). The inflammation recruits neutrophils, which release elastase and other proteases. These enzymes degrade the **elastic fibres** and **smooth muscle** of the bronchial walls. Over time, the airway loses its recoil and dilates permanently.

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- **Dilated bronchi** → **Tram-tracks on CXR** (thickened, parallel bronchial walls) and **air–fluid levels** on CT (dilated sacs).
- **Mucus stasis** → **Chronic cough with sputum** – the body tries to clear the accumulated secretions.
- **Neutrophillic inflammation** → **Coarse crackles** – secretions and airway wall thickening cause repetitive popping sounds during inspiration.
- **Bronchial artery hypertrophy** → **Haemoptysis** (especially in “dry” form) – fragile new vessels bleed easily.
- **Airflow obstruction** → **Dyspnoea** – from mucus plugging and airway collapse.
- **Repeated infections** → **Exacerbations** – bacterial overgrowth (often *Haemophilus influenzae*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*).
- **Progressive lung destruction** → **Chest deformity** (Harrison’s sulci in children) and **digital clubbing** (chronic infection).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | ~50% of cases | No clear cause after full workup; often bilateral lower lobe |
| **Post-infectious** | Severe pneumonia (TB, pertussis, measles, adenovirus) | History of pneumonia, TB contact, or childhood infection |
| **Immunodeficiency** | Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), IgG subclass deficiency | Recurrent sinopulmonary infections, low immunoglobulins |
| **Autoimmune / connective tissue disease** | Rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren’s, IB* | Arthritis, dry eyes/mouth, IBD |
| **Genetic** | *Cystic fibrosis (CF)* – most common genetic cause | Pancreatic insufficiency, male infertility, sweat test >60 mmol/L |
| **Ciliary dyskinesia** | Primary ciliary dyskinesia (e.g., Kartagener’s) | Situs inversus, infertility, chronic sinusitis |
| **Aspiration** | Recurrent aspiration (GERD, dysphagia) | History of stroke, dementia, hiatus hernia |
| **Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)** | Asthma with ABPA | High IgE, Aspergillus skin test positive, fleeting shadows on CXR |
| **Obstruction** | Lung mass, foreign body | Unilateral, focal bronchiectasis on CT |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (50%). In children and young adults, consider CF.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria:** Diagnosis is **radiologic** – high-resolution CT (HRCT) showing bronchiectasis (bronchial diameter > adjacent artery, lack of tapering, or visualisation of airways within 1 cm of pleural surface). There is no single scoring system; severity classified by number of lobes affected and presence of *Pseudomonas*.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **HRCT chest** | Bronchial dilation, bronchial wall thickening, mucus plugging | Gold standard to confirm diagnosis | Positive if bronchoarterial ratio >1 | 
| **Sputum culture** | Pathogens (e.g., *H. influenzae*, *P. aeruginosa*, *S. aureus*, NTM) | Guide antibiotic choice; identify colonisation | If growth of *P. aeruginosa* → worse prognosis, needs anti-pseudomonal regimen |
| **Chest X-ray** | Tram-tracks, ring shadows, volume loss | Initial screening (low sensitivity ~40%) | Normal CXR does not rule out bronchiectasis |
| **Full blood count** | Neutrophilia (infection), anaemia (chronic disease) | Exclude underlying anaemia; acute exacerbation | Raised WCC with neutrophilia suggests infection |
| **Immunoglobulins / B-cell subsets** | Low IgG (CVID), low subclasses | Detect immunodeficiency | IgG <4 g/L suggests CVID; IgG subclass deficiency common |
| **Sweat chloride** | Chloride >60 mmol/L | Screen for CF in adults <40 or young-onset | Abnormal → refer for CFTR genetics |
| **AE: CRP, blood cultures** | Raised CRP, bacteraemia | Assess severity of exacerbation | CRP >100 mg/L associated with poor outcome |
| **Lung function** | Obstructive pattern (reduced FEV1, FEV1/FVC <0.7) | Monitor disease progression | FEV1 decline >30 mL/year is rapid; correlates with mortality |
| **Bronchoscopy** (selective) | Visualises endobronchial obstruction, obtains BAL | If foreign body, tumour, or suspected ABPA | Lavage culture may isolate unsuspected NTM |

**Single finding that confirms:** HRCT showing bronchial dilation without tapering. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal HRCT chest (with no bronchiectasis in any lobe).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour) – acute severe exacerbation:**
- **Oxygen** – target SpO2 88–92% (avoid hyperoxia if at risk of hypercapnia).
- **Antibiotics** – if purulent sputum and fever:  
  - For mild exacerbation: *amoxicillin 500 mg PO TDS 7–10 days*  
  - If penicillin allergic: *doxycycline 200 mg PO stat, then 100 mg OD*  
  - If *Pseudomonas* known or recent (last culture): *ciprofloxacin 750 mg PO BD*  
- **Airway clearance** – start physiotherapy with active cycle of breathing (ACBT) **immediately** – do not wait for stabilisation.
- **Salbutamol nebulised 2.5–5 mg** – if wheeze or FEV1 response known.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – management of exacerbation and recovery:**
- Repeat sputum culture – adjust antibiotics based on sensitivities. If no improvement in 48 h, consider IV therapy: *ceftazidime 2 g IV TDS* or *piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS*.
- **Chest physiotherapy** TID – postural drainage, percussion, positive expiratory pressure (PEP) device.
- **Assess for complications:** persistent fever → infected cyst imaging repeat CXR; haemoptysis → consider bronchial artery embolisation if >300 mL/24h.
- **Treat underlying cause** – start *IVIG* if CVID; *corticosteroids* if ABPA (prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day tapering); *domperidone + PPIs* if aspiration.
- **NOT to do:** Do NOT give high-dose systemic steroids routinely (risk of infection augmentation). Do NOT use inhaled corticosteroids alone (no strong evidence, increased pneumonia risk).

**Long-term (chronic management):**
- **Airway clearance** – daily physiotherapy (PEP device or Autogenic drainage). Consider mucolytics: *carbocisteine 750 mg TDS* or *hypertonic saline 6% nebulised BD* if thick sputum.
- **Prophylactic antibiotics** – if ≥3 exacerbations/year:  
  - Rotating oral antibiotics (e.g., *azithromycin 250 mg PO three times per week*).  
  - Or inhaled *colistin* or *tobramycin* if *Pseudomonas* colonised.
- **Vaccinations** – annual influenza, pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPV23) once, and later PPV23 booster.
- **Surgery** – consider for localised bronchiectasis (e.g., post-obstructive) with recurrent severe infections despite medical therapy. Resection can cure if focal.
- **Monitor** – FEV1 annually, sputum cultures twice yearly, repeat HRCT if new symptoms.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Bronchiectasis | COPD | Asthma | Cystic Fibrosis |
|---------|----------------|------|--------|-----------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none; pleuritic if pneumothorax | Rare; tightness | Rare; chest tightness | None; sinus pain possible |
| **Exam** | Coarse crackles, clubbing (20%), wheeze (if obstruction) | Hyperinflation, wheeze, prolonged expiration | Expiratory wheeze, normal or hyperinflated | Crackles, clubbing, hyperinflation, nasal polyps |
| **CXR/CT** | Tram-tracks, ring shadows, air–fluid levels | Hyperinflation, bullae | Normal or hyperinflation | Bronchiectasis (upper lobe predominant), mucus plugging |
| **Sputum** | Purulent, daily, often positive for *H. influenzae* or *P. aeruginosa* | Mucoid, less volume, often sterile | Clear, scanty | Purulent, thick, often *S. aureus* or *P. aeruginosa* |
| **Allergy** | Not typical; ABPA can coexist | Not typical | Positive skin prick test, IgE | Not typical |
| **Key differentiator** | **HRCT shows bronchial dilation** | No bronchiectasis on HRCT; smoking hx | Variable symptoms, normal CT | **Sweat chloride >60**; CFTR mutation |
| **Response to steroids** | Minimal (unless ABPA) | Good (improves FEV1) | Excellent (reversible obstruction) | Minimal (unless ABPA) |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Exacerbation** | Increased cough, purulence, dyspnoea, fever | Worsening crackles, increased sputum volume, fever | Start antibiotics, airway clearance |
| **Massive haemoptysis** | Sudden coughing up >300 mL bright red blood | Hypoxaemia, hypotension, respiratory distress | Urgent bronchial artery embolisation (call interventional radiology), airway protection (intubation) |
| **Pneumothorax** | Acute pleuritic chest pain, dyspnoea | Unilateral reduced breath sounds, tracheal deviation if tension | Chest drain (small → aspirate; large → drain) |
| **Respiratory failure** | Severe dyspnoea, confusion, mottled skin | Low SpO2, high pCO2, acidosis | **Escalate to ICU** for non-invasive ventilation or mechanical ventilation |
| **Bronchiectasis-related lung abscess** | Persistent fever despite antibiotics, weight loss | Localised crackles, HRCT shows cavity | CT-guided drainage or surgical lobectomy |
| **Cor pulmonale** | Fatigue, peripheral oedema, raised JVP | Right ventricular heave, loud P2, hepatomegaly | Diuretics, oxygen therapy, treat underlying lung disease |
| **Pulmonary hypertension** | Exertional syncope, chest pain, haemoptysis | Loud P2, right axis deviation on ECG | Echocardiogram; refer to specialist |
| **Death** | Progressive decline, recurrent severe exacerbations | Cachexia, low FEV1, frequent hospitalisation | Palliative care; consider lung transplant |

**Late-presentation patterns – “what happens if untreated”:**
- Untreated massive haemoptysis → exsanguination → death.
- Repeated exacerbations without airway clearance → progressive loss of lung function → respiratory failure.
- Missed underlying immunodeficiency → recurrent infections → permanent lung damage.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
1. **“BRONCHIECTASIS”** – for causes:  
   - **B** – Bronchial obstruction (tumour, foreign body)  
   - **R** – Rheumatoid / RA  
   - **O** – Obstruction (post-infectious)  
   - **N** – NTM (non-tuberculous mycobacteria)  
   - **C** – Cystic fibrosis  
   - **H** – Hypogammaglobulinaemia  
   - **I** – Idiopathic (most common)  
   - **E** – Eing (ABPA)  
   - **C** – Ciliary dyskinesia  
   - **T** – TB  
   - **A** – Aspiration  
   - **S** – Swyer-James syndrome  
   - **I** – Irritants (toxic gases)  
   - **S** – Sternomastoid? (just a filler, but remember idiopathic)
2. **“PILLAR”** – for management pillars:  
   - **P** – Physiotherapy (airway clearance)  
   - **I** – Infective exacerbations (treat early)  
   - **L** – Lifestyle (vaccinations, exercise)  
   - **L** – Lung damage monitoring (HRCT, FEV1)  
   - **A** – Antibiotics (prophylactic if needed)  
   - **R** – Referral (surgery, transplant)

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **“Dry” bronchiectasis → think TB** – fibrosis and traction bronchiectasis in upper lobes.
2. **Clubbing + crackles** = think bronchiectasis (clubbing rare in uncomplicated COPD).
3. **Sputum colour: “pea soup” green** suggests *Pseudomonas*.
4. **Recurrent pneumonia same lobe** → think focal bronchiectasis from obstruction.
5. **CT shows “signet rings”** (dilated bronchus next to pulmonary artery) – pathognomonic.
6. **Inhaled corticosteroids increase pneumonia risk** – avoid in bronchiectasis without asthma/ABPA.
7. **Ciprofloxacin** is the only oral anti-pseudomonal – use sparingly; resistance develops fast.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Missing bronchiectasis as a cause of “COPD exacerbation” → fails to initiate airway clearance.
- Treating exacerbations with amoxicillin alone when *Pseudomonas* is known → leads to failure.
- Forgetting to culture sputum before antibiotics → cannot target therapy.
- Neglecting physiotherapy → patient stales and re-admission.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
| Parameter | When to escalate | To whom | Action |
|-----------|------------------|---------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Any single red (respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92% on room air, or any oxygen) | Senior clinician / med reg | Immediate review, consider escalation |
| **GCS <15** | Drowsiness, confusion – may be hypercapnic (pCO2 >6 kPa) | ICU outreach / respiratory team | Check ABG, start NIV if pCO2 >6 + pH <7.35 |
| **Massive haemoptysis** | >300 mL/24h or any haemoptysis with hypotension | Interventional radiology (embolisation) | Organise CT angiography; call IR; prepare for intubation |
| **Lactate >2 mmol/L** | Sepsis from pneumonia or lung abscess | ICU | Start IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, consider critical care |
| **Respiratory arrest** | Apnoea, gasping, bradycardia | Crash team (2222) | Start resuscitation, airway support |
| **Pneumothorax (tension)** | Unilateral hyper-resonance + tracheal deviation + hypotension | Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL) | Then chest drain |

**General rule:** Any patient with bronchiectasis who has a sudden deterioration in oxygen saturations or new confusion needs **urgent ABG** and **senior review** – hypercapnic respiratory failure can be silent.
